title: {key:gx} and {key:g<}
subtitle: Open URLs and recall command output.
summary: gx opens the URL or filename under cursor in the system handler (browser/etc.). g< re-displays the output of the last :command — handy when a long message scrolled past.

blocks.text: Two small {key:g} commands worth knowing — both solve real problems.

{key:g}{key:x} on a URL or file path passes it to the system's default opener (`open` on macOS, `xdg-open` on Linux, `cmd /c start` on Windows). Browsers open URLs; PDFs open in PDF readers; image files open in image viewers.

blocks.title: How gx Decides What to Open
blocks.text:
The implementation is in `$VIMRUNTIME/plugin/netrwPlugin.vim` — Vim's netrw plugin defines `gx` as a Normal-mode mapping that calls `netrw#BrowseX(netrw#GX(), 0)`.

The URL/path is found by looking at the WORD under the cursor (not just word) — so URLs with `.`, `?`, `&`, etc. are captured whole. The handler is selected by `'browser'` if set, otherwise by platform defaults.
{key:g}{key:<} reopens the message area showing the output of the *last* Ex command — handy when a `:registers` or `:set all` listing scrolled past faster than you could read.

blocks.title: Where Messages Live
blocks.text:
Vim keeps a rolling buffer of message-area output. `:messages` shows the recent history; {key:g}{key:<} brings back the most recent block.

After {key:g}{key:<}, the message hit-Enter prompt appears so you can scroll the listing with {key:Space}/{key:b}, then dismiss with {key:Enter}.
